meta.title: This House rejects political alignment as a growth strategy for crypto.

transcript:
Miller Whitehouse-Levine: My name is Miller Whitehouse-Levine with the Solana Policy Institute, and this House rejects political alignment as a growth strategy for crypto for four core reasons. Uh, I'd like to introduce myself before getting to them though. Uh, again, my name is Miller Whitehouse-Levine, the Solana Policy Institute advocates on behalf of anyone working in the Solana ecosystem in Washington D.C. and soon to be around the world. If you have policy, regulatory problems or opportunities, we're here to help you address them. Uh, which brings me to my first point. Policy, politics is relevant to crypto because crypto has been successful and has grown so much. Innovation drives growth, policy and politics does not. Crypto is relevant in these, uh, debates—this debate is happening here on this stage—because of the products and the protocols that the folks in this room have built. D.C., policymakers, laws, uh, have—are not generally the source of wonders and innovations that make the world a better place. In fact, they can be a major hindrance, and, uh, uh, that is generally their role in society. I would—I would hedge that a little bit and say it's, uh, they're designed to address negative externalities of growth, but again, crypto matters because it solves real-world problems, not because any politician or policymaker said it matters. Number two, I think aligning with any political agenda undermines crypto's promise to everyone in the world. Crypto is for everyone. It's not for any individual cohort of individuals or a specific type of person. Crypto is here to empower people to engage in transactions, custody their own assets, no matter their creed or code, and aligning with any specific political agenda undermines crypto's core promise. Number three, crypto is global, obviously. We're here in Abu Dhabi. I'm an American. I think we've probably have over a hundred nationalities represented at Breakpoint this year. And crypto is here, uh, again for everyone. If we as an industry align with any, uh, in particular political system or political agenda, we necessarily, uh, uh, exclude others from that, uh, innovation and those benefits. So I think crypto, uh, should not align with any specific agenda because it's global, and, uh, alignment in any specific area will necessarily make the tent smaller, so to speak, internationally. Finally, and I think perhaps most importantly, crypto is here to build a new technology and a new financial system for the next few centuries, not the next few years. Politics is too ephemeral for us to align with any particular political agenda and expect long-term growth. I think that if crypto, for example, adopted a, uh, political platform that was out of power in a few years, uh, we would come to regret it because there will be an equal and opposite reaction that will try and, uh, you know, punish us for our alignment with any in particular—excuse me—specific political agenda. So to recap, this House rejects political alignment as a growth strategy for crypto because, uh-oh, someone didn't like that.

Greg Xethalis: Yes, point of order.

Miller Whitehouse-Levine: Point of order.

Greg Xethalis: Point of personal privilege and order. Uh, I want to—I—I let you get through your arguments first because I think they are valuable to hear.

Miller Whitehouse-Levine: Thank you, Greg.

Greg Xethalis: But I think it is reflective that we're here in Abu Dhabi with a global community, and yet we have invited to this stage three US lawyers and one US policy expert. And the reason for that is while crypto is global, unfortunate or not, the United States is the only country in the world that thinks its borders know no ends. So it is important to get it right in the US, not only because of the Sovereign District of New York and the overreach of US laws with global extraterritoriality, but also because US policy often drives global policy.

Miller Whitehouse-Levine: Greg, I accept but reject that point because I think, uh, the evidence bears out that you're wrong. Between 2021 and 2024, US policy was extraordinarily hostile to crypto, as I'm sure you would, uh, you would acknowledge, and nevertheless, crypto grew significantly over that time period, and in fact grew more than it has over the last 12 months. So I think that, uh, the evidence bears out that, uh, alignment with any particular political agenda doesn't move the needle in the way that, uh, you're representing it does. Thank you, uh, for your time.

Marco Santori: Oh, my sweet summer child. What you have just heard is a remarkably naive view on politics. And how far we must have fallen since 2012 when I mined my own first Bitcoin. We've been fighting for individual rights and sovereignty, the freedom to transact since that day. And what you heard here is encouragement to not get involved, to back away, to step down from that fight. And I... Miller, I say this with love, um, I really do wish the world worked the way in which you just described. I—I want a world in which industries can simply stay above politics. I want a world where regulators can behave predictably, uh, where governments just craft clear rules without anybody fighting for them. But that's not the world we live in. And after 14 years of litigating, negotiating, and surviving, to a certain extent, crypto policy, I can tell you with a little bit of confidence that neutrality is not a protection strategy. It's a permission slip for others to define your future. Let me start with a core misconception in the "stay apolitical" frame. Political alignment is not partnership—it's, I should say, political alignment is not partisanship. It's not choosing sides. It's choosing principles. Crypto must articulate those principles because industries that fail to do that have those principles articulated for them. For them, by their adversaries, in fact. And we now have in multiple jurisdictions political actors who this day, right now as we listen to each other talk, are fundraising against us. They are advocating against us in darker rooms than these. More political rooms, indeed. Now, Greg and I have three decades of combined experience across the US, in Europe, in emerging political markets, and here's what we've learned: every single region has its center of political gravity. And crypto has to engage where that gravity is strongest. If we don't, we all get pulled into it. We don't define it. I want to come back to Miller's point that political alignment could be dangerous because it risks—it risks partisanship. And if we give power to one side, it might be used against us when another side takes hold and takes power. Well, I agree. That's right. There's a risk. But only if you do it badly. There's always a risk in engaging. And if you don't engage, yeah, you don't take risks, but you can't win the rewards either. I think principles-based alignment is the opposite of regulatory capture. I think it's how you recruit across ideologies, and we're already seeing that in practice. Across multiple countries, including the US, crypto has supporters on the left, it has supporters on the right. I've actually been on both sides myself, and even in the center. But in the past year, dozens of Democratic legislators, for example, have actually voted for crypto-friendly market structure frameworks. That doesn't happen by accident. That happens because, well, we engaged. Because we explained. Because we won them over. We decided at the outset they weren't enemies; they were just friends we hadn't made yet. So yeah, I think it would be lovely if crypto could just be apolitical. But that world doesn't exist in the United States, it doesn't exist in Europe, it doesn't exist anywhere. I think crypto grows when it engages, and I think it shrinks when it hides. So yeah, political engagement can be risky, but it's more than just a risk. I think it's a strategy that makes every other strategy possible. Thank you.

Rebecca Rettig: Thanks everybody. Uh, I was a litigator, so got up in court and argued my position for many years before I got into crypto. And I will tell you, Marco did what every good litigator does. He redefines the question because he knows he can't win on his side of the argument. The question was not, "Oh, should there be political engagement?" If you look at what's up there, the question is, "Should you have political alignment?" And alignment means, should you pick one side or the other? Honestly, Marco, take a seat over here because you did a great job advocating for our position. But I'm going to engage on the substance and with the question that was actually presented to us. And I was going to go point by point through what I thought Marco was going to say, uh, and rebut what he had to say, but I can't because he said, uh, neutrality is the way to go and engagement is the way to go, and we agree. But let's go through what, uh, the real questions are today. If you pick one side, you have to know that every action has an equal and opposite reaction. So if we align with one side in any country and with one political party or political position at any time, you are going to have a strong and forceful backlash. And I'm going to use the example of the UK and the US to contrast each other. And what I'm going to tell you is actually going to surprise you because I think you're going to expect me to say something about the US that is different. But in the UK, we had very, very strong political alignment, um, through Rishi Sunak, and crypto came to the UK in droves. That was going to be our answer. They were going to push the US, which was very hostile in the last administration to crypto. People opened new offices, they made big splashes, they really engaged with the regulators there. There were lots of meetings at 10 Downing. Rishi's out of power now, and what's happened in the UK? Policy for crypto is slowly making its way through. What did we do during the last four years in the administration when crypto was extremely painful in the US? We engaged on both sides. We engaged and we engaged neutrally, Marco and Greg—I know you're coming here. And what's the result of that? We have the FIT21 Act in the United States. And if we had not engaged on both sides, we would have never been able to have stablecoin legislation, Democrats voting for it, and even though there was a lot of resistance on many sides—on both sides of the aisle—we engaged on both sides. The other—the next point is that the day-to-day reality of policy and law is completely untethered with politics. And the people that we are writing policy with—one, the staffers on the Hill, or the people in the regulatory agencies, whether it be in the US, the UK, Japan, wherever we may be—I am engaging every day with the same regulators I was four years ago. Uh, and it doesn't matter who's political in power; you need to continue to engage with those people. So politics may set a tone, but the implementation is apolitical. Three, crypto is bigger than politics. It is durable, as Miller said, so I won't belabor it. Uh, crypto grew immensely between 2021 and 2024, and that was a very, very painful time. There was a lot of political pressure against moving both in dark rooms and out in the light. It was politically attractive to be anti-crypto, and we all painfully engaged on both sides of the aisle. Uh, crypto is bigger than one moment in time. It grows and it changes. Where we are today is not where crypto is going to be a year from now, six months from now, a year from now, six years from now. And we are lucky that we have all the builders in the room today who are going to keep pushing crypto forward. So engaging and picking a political side today is not going to allow our builders to continue on. And, uh, sentiment in crypto is completely uncorrelated with politics. Tokens are down, and we have a lot of really exciting building going on. We have a lot of really exciting political momentum. And so what's happening on the ground in crypto isn't what's happening on the political front. And so picking who loves us the most at one moment in time is not going to move us forward. So I submit we should, uh, reject political alignment as a growth strategy for crypto.

Greg Xethalis: Thank you, Rebecca, and I also congratulate you on also not really discussing the topic for about half of the time you spoke. Uh, but I do want to say you are right about one thing, which is political engagement does come with risks and the potential for backlash. But what you've just described isn't a roadmap; it's a retreat. And retreat is not a strategy for an industry already living under policy fire. Let me start with the claim that I really take issue with: that political alignment can create backlash. Backlash? Backlash is what happens when you don't engage. It's what happens when regulators have mandates from a small subset of politicians to set narratives in a vacuum that we refuse to fill. And backlash is a feature of disengagement, not a bug of alignment. And this idea that we're inviting politics into crypto—politics arrived years ago. We're just choosing to stop pretending that it's in the room with us—that it's not in the room with us. And political alignment is not how crypto became partisan; it's how crypto became legible. So let me be clear: crypto did not politicize itself; politics politicized crypto. One small faction of one party literally fundraises on being anti-crypto. They say they're building an anti-crypto army. That's not a hypothetical risk; that's a budget item—budget line item in a political campaign. And if a political actor is raising money by attacking your industry, staying above the fray is not a philosophy. It's an engraved invitation to keep hitting you. So if your playbook is "take the punches, hope they get bored, and pray for technocrats to swoop in with perfect rules," I promise you this: that is not how industries survive. It's how they get written out of the future. Here is the truth: you can't de-escalate a fight you didn't start. So alignment is not partisanship; it's insulation. You know, you might warn about political capture, and so do we. That's exactly why the alignment that all four of us have been doing in D.C. is built around principles and not parties. It's pro-innovation, pro-open systems, pro-consumer protection through market transparency, and anti-regulation by enforcement. If you articulate those principles clearly, they resonate across ideologies and across sides of the aisle. And we've already seen it, as Marco mentioned: dozens of Democrats voted for clarity, voted for the CRA, and voted for even anti-CBDC—maybe a few fewer there. But that is not partisanship; that's coalition building. And the best way to keep crypto from becoming partisan is to actually build that bipartisan coalition, not wishing for it by staying silent. Because silence is not neutrality. Silence is consent to be defined by your loudest critics. And backlash isn't caused by political alignment; it's caused by absence. You know, you might say, "If we get involved politically, we'll trigger negative reactions." No, the negative reactions already happened, and that's why we are political. They happened when regulators declared tokens securities without rulemaking, when enforcement replaced policy, and when entire ecosystems were denied banking services and innovators were treated as hostile actors via subpoena. Not talking about politics didn't prevent any of it; it allowed it. And we're stopping it today by talking about politics. We didn't land on Capitol Hill; Capitol Hill landed on us. So political alignment is how you build shock absorbers, not how you provoke earthquakes. And finally, political alignment is how you turn principles into policy. Rebecca just painted, and Miller as well, painted alignment as a liability. But the greatest liability in a democracy is a vacuum. Responsibility flows to the people who organize. Authority flows to the people who show up. And clarity flows to the industries that make their principles legible to policymakers. We want predictable rules, regulatory boundaries, operational certainty, global competitiveness, and consumer protection based on transparency and verification. Those are not fringe demands, and they don't belong to any party, but we must fight those who oppose it. And that would be the foundation of every modern financial system, including our own. And political alignment, in fact, is the bridge between principle and law. So clarity requires legislation, legislation requires coalitions, and coalitions rely on engagement and political alignment. There is no technocratic shortcut. If you don't articulate your values, someone else will articulate their fears about you. And guess what? They have. So yes, political engagement has risks, but disengagement has consequences, and we've felt those. Crypto grows when it defines its principles and organizes around them, and crypto shrinks when it hides. Thank you, Rebecca. Lovely platitudes. Um, let me actually bring it back to where you guys began. You've said that political alignment is risky, it's partisan and divisive. It doesn't have to be. And what we've argued today is the opposite. Political alignment done right is how you avoid partisanship, not how you create it. They may like to frame the question a particular way, but that's not the reality that we've lived on a day-to-day basis. Crypto isn't picking a side; it's aligning around principles. It's the principles that allow all of you to experience this industry, build it, and grow it. And those principles have champions in both parties, yes. And they have some enemies on the Hill as well. And aligning against them is what is important. And, you know, you've both—particularly you with the ear—you were really talking about education, talking to everyone. We've—Miller—I mean, Marco and I have been educating people on crypto for 14 years. I had the first meetings at the SEC on crypto. Went into banks, went into lawmakers, went into regulators. Well, I'll tell you the lesson I've learned is that education without political leverage produces exactly what we've had for the last four years: innovation shaped by subpoenas and settlements rather than statutes. And warning that alignment could backfire? The real backfire again is pretending that neutrality protects us when a faction of one party is openly hostile. Again, silence is not a strategy. Silence is surrender on the political front, no matter what we do as builders and educators. In Europe, technocrats write the first draft of policy. In the United States, where we operate, political—politics write it. And if we want clear market structure rules, like we got clear stablecoin rules, we need to have political engagement to get them. And political engagement takes alignment among everyone in this room, including you two. That's it. Thank you all.

# House Republicans revive criticisms of Biden's 'coordinated attack' on crypto, debanking concerns

By [Sarah Wynn](https://www.theblock.co/author/sarah-wynn)

[Policy](https://www.theblock.co/category/policy)
• December 1, 2025, 1:36PM EST

Share

Partner offers

The Block may may earn a commission if you use our partner offers, at no extra cost to you.

Start trading cryptoGet a crypto rewards card

#### Quick Take

- Republicans on the House Financial Services Committee released a 53-page report on Monday titled, “Operation Choke Point 2.0: Biden’s Debanking of Digital Assets.”
- This is now the second crypto-related report to come out of Congress over the past few days as lawmakers press on to advance legislation to regulate the crypto industry at large.

House Republicans criticized what they described as the Biden administration’s "coordinated attack" on digital assets and urged regulators and fellow lawmakers to take steps to ensure the industry is not shunned from the financial system in the future.

Republicans on the House Financial Services Committee released a 53-page report on Monday titled "Operation Choke Point 2.0: Biden's Debanking of Digital Assets." The document stems from a probe started over the last few years into the previous administration's crypto policies.

"The Biden Administration sought to make it nearly impossible to engage in digital asset-related activities," they said in the report.

Republicans on the House Financial Services Committee said they are aiming to ensure that a third version of "Operation Choke Point" won't happen again. The phrase Operation Choke Point 2.0 was coined by Castle Island Ventures co-founder Nic Carter in 2023, drawing comparisons to the Obama-era effort to limit banking services for sectors viewed as high-risk, such as firearm dealers.

Fears that crypto participants are again being denied banking services have surfaced again [recently](https://www.theblock.co/post/380099/strike-jack-maller-jpmorgan) after Strike CEO Jack Mallers said JPMorgan Chase closed his personal accounts without a proper explanation.

Much of the congressional report on Monday focused on previous actions and statements made by regulators, including the former Securities and Exchange Commission Chair Gary Gensler's assertion that most cryptocurrencies are securities that drove the agency's “enforce first, make rules never" strategy.

tags.impact_reason: While the Solana Policy Institute's stance on political alignment is relevant for the ecosystem's long-term strategy, it doesn't represent a groundbreaking development or immediate shift in the Solana landscape.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose a neoclassical marble pillar as the Semantic Anchor because it represents the established legal and political institutions of Washington D.C. referenced in the story ('Legal Heavyweights,' 'policymakers,' 'Washington'). The core conflict is the 'rejection of neutrality' for a more 'aggressive political alignment.' The visual of a monumental pillar, engraved with the word 'NEUTRALITY' but shattered by a crack, directly narrates this strategic failure and 'militant pivot.' The digital light erupting from within symbolizes crypto's force being unleashed in this new confrontational manner.
visual_idea.description: A macro close-up on the fractured marble pillar. The shot focuses on the rough, detailed texture of the crack. On either side of the dark fissure, we see fragments of the chiseled word, perhaps 'TRAL' and 'ITY'. Energetic, glowing purple and green light pours out from the crack, illuminating the sharp edges of the broken stone and hinting at the powerful digital force within.

final_prompt.prompt_text: A stunning macro close-up, 1:1 aspect ratio, perfectly centered, filling the frame with intricate texture. The subject is a grand fractured marble pillar, showcasing its rough, detailed texture and neoclassical architectural elegance even in disrepair. A deep, dark fissure dramatically runs through the marble. On either side of this crack, fragments of chiseled letters are visibly embedded into the stone: 'TRAL' on one side and 'ITY' on the other, hinting at a powerful, ancient inscription. From within the dark fissure, an energetic, glowing purple and green digital light intensely pours out, acting as the primary light source. This light creates a dramatic chiaroscuro lighting effect, casting sharp, brilliant highlights on the broken, fractured edges of the marble and generating deep, contrasting shadows that emphasize the pillar's volumetric details and rough texture. The vibrant digital energy emanating from the crack powerfully illuminates the rough, broken stone, revealing every minute detail of its texture and hinting at a contained, otherworldly force. The composition is symmetrical, with a central focus on the illuminated crack and the surrounding textured marble, presented with tight macro framing. 8k, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, volumetric lighting, subsurface scattering.

editorial.headline: Legal Heavyweights Reject Neutrality as Crypto Growth Strategy
editorial.standfirst: A decisive shift in institutional strategy sees industry leaders abandoning "educational" diplomacy for aggressive political alignment. The debate exposes the failure of apolitical engagement to prevent regulatory enforcement, signaling a militant pivot in Washington.
key_takeaways: Marco Santori and Greg Xethalis argue "neutrality is a permission slip" for regulators, citing the failure of the 2021-2024 education-first approach to stop subpoenas.
Rebecca Rettig warns that political alignment creates volatility, citing the UK's crypto stall after the exit of Rishi Sunak as proof of the risks of partisan betting.
The Solana Policy Institute maintains that innovation, not policy, drives growth, noting that crypto expanded significantly despite the hostile "Operation Choke Point 2.0" environment.
editorial.article_body_markdown:
The era of the "apolitical technocrat" is effectively over. For years, the crypto industry operated under the delusion that superior code would inevitably lead to favorable law, a strategy that **Marco Santori** and **Greg Xethalis** dismantled on the Breakpoint stage. The consensus among the aggressive faction is clear: the "education-only" strategy utilized between 2021 and 2024 failed to prevent the **SEC**'s regulation-by-enforcement campaign. As Xethalis noted, "silence is consent," and the industry’s refusal to engage in political trench warfare allowed narratives to be written by its adversaries.

However, the risks of abandoning neutrality are not theoretical. **Rebecca Rettig** provided the critical counter-weight, illustrating the dangers of tethering an immutable technology to ephemeral political administrations. The collapse of the UK's crypto ambitions following the departure of **Rishi Sunak** serves as a stark warning: political patronage is a liability when power changes hands. The **Solana Policy Institute** reinforced this, arguing that aligning with a specific agenda alienates global users and that the industry's most significant growth occurred during periods of intense US hostility, suggesting a low correlation between D.C. sentiment and on-chain adoption.

The debate marks a maturing of the industry's "Foreign Policy." The naive hope for a "technocratic savior" has been replaced by a pragmatic understanding of leverage. While **Miller Whitehouse-Levine** argues that protocols solve problems politicians cannot, the legal reality is that without political cover—specifically the kind built through the **FIT21 Act** coalitions—innovation remains under threat of subpoena. The industry is no longer asking for permission; it is organizing to ensure it no longer needs to.
